title: Chapter 61: Sera's Choice
content: The C-130 Hercules sat on the tarmac like a beached whale, its khaki paintjob scabbed with rust and repaired patches that told the story of a hundred desperate evacuations. Aviation fuel — siphoned from reserves the Baroness had cached across three counties — flowed through feeder lines into its tanks with the slow, agonizing patience of a blood transfusion.
Marcus watched from the hangar door, arms crossed, jaw working.

Behind him, the airport terminal had been converted into a staging ground. Crates of medical supplies. Boxes of preserved food. Canisters of water purification tablets. Everything the Taipei survivors would need to implement the cure, bundled and weighed and manifest-listed by Vivienne with the precision of a woman who'd run logistics for a shadow government.
But the cargo wasn't the problem.
The crew was.
"Fuel's at sixty percent and climbing," called a voice from the wing.

Captain Jiro Tanaka climbed down from the cargo bay with the loose-limbed ease of a man who'd been getting in and out of military aircraft since before the Deadrose. He was compact, mid-forties, with a weathered face that cratered into a smile the moment he saw Marcus.

"Mr. Chen. We meet again." He extended a hand — callused, steady, the hand of someone who could land a transport plane on a cratered runway with crosswinds. "I heard you were the one who arranged this little field trip."
"Captain." Marcus shook the hand. "I owe you for getting us this far."

"You owe me nothing. I was paid in antibiotics and the continued use of my spinal column." Tanaka's grin didn't falter. "Though I have to ask — any relation to the Tanaka who runs the communications tower? Junko?"
Marcus blinked. "No. Different Tanaka. Completely unrelated."
"That's what she said too." Tanaka chuckled. "Half the survivors on the East Coast claim some Tanaka connection. It's like Smith, but with more honorifics."
"It's going to be a running joke, isn't it."
"Oh, absolutely. I've got material for the whole flight."
Marcus almost smiled. Almost.
The humor drained from the moment Sera stepped onto the tarmac.

She came through the hangar door with Lily at her side, the two of them a study in contrasts — Lily pale and sharp and wound-tight like a mainspring, Sera dark and still and radiating something that made the nearest guards unconsciously straighten their spines. The Queen Authority. Even when she wasn't using it, the psychic pressure of her presence reshaped the space around her.
She met Marcus's eyes, and through the Bond, he felt it.
*Let me come.*

The words weren't spoken. They didn't need to be. The Bond carried them with the clarity of a shout in an empty cathedral — her desperation, her need, the fierce protective instinct that had nothing to do with Marcus's physical safety and everything to do with what lived inside his chest.
His parents were his heart. And his heart was her territory.
"Sera," he said aloud. "We need to talk about—"

"I'm going." She said it flatly. Not a debate. A declaration. "Your parents are in Taipei. I can help. My authority over the dead extends further every day — if there are zombies at Taoyuan, I can clear the runway without a single shot."
"Sera."
"You need me."
"Sera." He stepped closer, pitching his voice low. "You're the only thing holding the moat together."
She flinched. He felt it through the Bond — a white-hot spike of frustration that bled into his own chest like sympathetic vibration.

The zombie moat. The ring of controlled undead that encircled New Eden, standing motionless in concentric rings for kilometers in every direction, a living wall of flesh that no raider army and no feral horde had breached since the day Sera claimed them. Twenty thousand corpses, give or take, all bound to her will.
All bound to *her*. Not to a system. Not to a protocol. To Sera. To her presence. To the constant, low-frequency broadcast of Queen Authority that kept them docile and directed.
If she left, the signal went silent.
And the moat would remember it was hungry.
Lily stepped forward, hands clasped in front of her like a student addressing a teacher. "I'll go."
Both of them looked at her.

"I have the Cupid data," Lily continued, her voice pitched a half-octave higher than normal — nervous, but determined. "All of it. The synthesis protocols, the dosing schedules, the distribution framework. I designed half the briefing decks myself. I can walk the Taipei survivors through every step."

"Lily." Marcus kept his voice gentle. "The flight is twelve hours over open ocean. We could hit turbulence, mechanical failure, aerial infected — you've seen what the carrion birds do to small aircraft. And Taipei is an unknown. We don't know what's waiting on the other end."
"I know."
"You're not a fighter."

"I'm not." She swallowed. "But I'm the best person to deliver the cure data. And I'm— I'm expendable, Marcus. In a way that she isn't." She didn't look at Sera when she said it. "If I die, the cure survives in the data stores. If she dies, the entire defensive perimeter of New Eden collapses and everyone here dies. The math isn't hard."
The silence that followed was the texture of broken glass.
Sera's hand closed around Lily's wrist. Not hard. Not possessive. Just — *there*. Through the Bond, Marcus felt the surge of protectiveness that shot through Sera like a current.
"You're not expendable," Sera said quietly. "Don't ever say that."
"Sera—"
"You're my *sister*."

The word hung in the air between them. Lily's eyes went glassy. She blinked rapidly, once, twice, and then her jaw set with the same stubborn resolve that Marcus had seen in Sera a hundred times. Genetics or environment — the Vale sisters had the same fire in them, even if one of them channeled it through numbers and the other through armies of the dead.
Before anyone could speak again, Sera went rigid.

Marcus felt it through the Bond like a thunderclap — a sudden spike of alarm that lit up every nerve in his body. Sera's head snapped toward the northwest, toward the skeletal skyline of downtown where the skyscrapers stood like broken teeth against the amber sky.
"What is it?" Marcus grabbed her arm.
"Something—" She pressed her hand to her temple. Her pupils dilated. "Something's *waking up*."

Through the Bond, Marcus felt it too. A distant pressure, like a change in barometrics before a hurricane. Something vast and cold and *hungry* was stirring in the ruins of the old city. Something that didn't move like the others, didn't think like the others. Something that was pulling itself together from the chaos with deliberation, with *purpose*.
An Alpha.
A *new* Alpha.

"There's been one forming for days," Sera whispered, her voice distant, her consciousness stretched toward the disturbance like a hand reaching into dark water. "I felt it growing but I thought — I thought I had more time. It's consolidating the ferals in the old district. Pulling them into a network. A competing network."
"If you leave," Marcus said, the words tasting like ash, "it takes over your zombies."

"If I leave, I lose the moat within hours. Maybe less." Her hand dropped from her temple. Her eyes focused on Marcus with terrible clarity. "And then there's nothing between the Alpha's army and New Eden but a chain-link fence and some very tired people with guns."
The implications spread out like cracks in ice.

No moat. No buffer. The Baroness's mercenaries were good, but they weren't *twenty-thousand-undead* good. The civilians inside New Eden — the children, the elderly, the researchers working on mass-producing the cure — they'd be overrun in a night.
Sera closed her eyes.
Through the Bond, Marcus felt her *choosing*.

It wasn't a single decision. It was a fractal — a thousand small surrenders collapsing into one terrible acceptance. She wanted to go. Every fiber of her being wanted to go. Saving his parents was saving him, and saving him was saving herself, and the Bond sang with the rightness of it, the logic of protection and partnership and *ownership* —
And she chose to stay.
Not because he asked. Not because it was safe. Not because the Bond compelled her to preserve herself for his sake.
Because Lily was here.
Because New Eden was full of people who had no one else.
Because being a queen meant standing between your people and annihilation, even when your heart was climbing onto a cargo plane without you.
She opened her eyes. They were dry. Clear. Absolutely certain.
"Go save your parents."
Her voice didn't waver.

She stepped forward and pressed her forehead to his — the gesture that had become theirs, intimate and grounding, the Bond made physical. Through the contact, Marcus felt everything she wasn't saying: the grief, the longing, the iron will that was holding both at bay.
"I'll be here when you get back."

**[SYSTEM NOTIFICATION]**
**AFFECTION: Seraphina — 90 → 95**
**Bond Achievement Unlocked: Selfless Sacrifice**
*The highest expression of the Bond is not possession — it is choosing your partner's good above your own desire.*

Marcus cradled her face in his hands. He felt the Bond humming between them, warm and golden and alive, and he knew — with the bone-deep certainty that came from surviving the end of the world together — that this might be the last time.
He kissed her.

Not softly. Not gently. He kissed her with the weight of everything they'd built and everything they stood to lose, with the desperate faith of a man who was boarding a plane to war and leaving half his soul on the tarmac. She kissed him back with equal ferocity, her fingers curling into the front of his jacket, and for one infinite moment the airport and the plane and the waiting Alpha and the hungry dead all fell away and there was only the two of them, only the Bond, only the stubborn, reckless, impossible love that had somehow taken root in the ruins of civilization.
Then he let her go.
"On the plane, Mr. Chen," Captain Tanaka called from the cockpit, his voice stripped of humor. "We've got a weather window and I intend to use it."

Vivienne was already aboard, running through preflight checklists with the two mercenaries — a stocky woman named Morales and a hollow-eyed man called Griggs — stowing weapons and securing cargo. Lily stood at the base of the cargo ramp, watching Sera with an expression that lived somewhere between grief and awe.
"Go," Sera told her sister. "Make sure they understand the cure. Every detail."
"I will." Lily's voice cracked. "I love you."
"I love you too. Now get on the plane before I change my mind and chain you to the radio tower."
Lily laughed — wet, broken — and climbed the ramp.
Marcus stopped at the top. Turned back.

Sera stood alone on the tarmac, the wind pulling at her hair, her posture straight as a blade. Behind her, the airport terminal rose in blocky silhouette against the darkening sky. Beyond that, invisible but present, twenty thousand dead things waited for their queen's command.
She didn't wave.
She didn't cry.
She was the Zombie Queen, and Zombie Queens did not weep on tarmacs.
The cargo ramp began to rise. Marcus watched her figure shrink as the hydraulic door swallowed the world until there was nothing but metal walls and the smell of aviation fuel.
The C-130's engines coughed, roared, and settled into a shuddering crescendo. The plane rolled forward, gathering speed along the cracked runway, the landing gear bouncing over patched concrete.
From the airport control tower, Sera watched it go.

The plane accelerated — sixty knots, eighty, a hundred — and then the nose lifted and it was airborne, climbing into the amber sky on a pillar of noise and exhaust, banking west toward the open Pacific and the twelve-hour flight to Taipei.

She watched until it was a dark cross against the clouds. Then her hand fell from the railing and she turned away, toward the northwest, toward the cold new pressure that was growing in the bones of the city.
The Alpha was waking.
Her zombies shivered in their concentric rings, sensing the shift.
Sera Vale lifted her chin and squared her shoulders, and walked back toward New Eden with the steady purpose of a woman who had chosen exactly who she was going to be.
She didn't cry.

But three kilometers away, at eight thousand feet and climbing, Marcus Chen pressed his hand to the window and felt, through the Bond that distance could not sever, a single tear fall against her cheek.
*End of Chapter 61*
